generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches as though it has been carved by the same storm that now roils the skies above me. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old and cruel companion, roars in defiance of the heavens. I can feel it pressing against the stone walls of this lighthouse, as if it too knows that something is ending. Perhaps it does.

You were never meant to leave, my child. You were born to be here, with me, in this quiet, lonely place where the world forgets its name. But you did. You walked away, and now I am left with only the sea, the light, and the ghosts of your voice.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not one word. I tried, once, to write, but the letters always ended up in the fire, as though the very act of writing to you was a betrayal. And perhaps it was. Perhaps it still is.

I have not forgotten you, though. Not a single day. I see you in the flicker of the lamp, in the way the light dances across the waves, in the way the wind whispers your name through the cracks in the stone. You are here, even when you are not. And I am left to bear the weight of what I could not say, what I could not do.

I have spent these years watching the sea

My dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very sky. I have not seen the stars in days, and yet I feel as though I am gazing at them through a veil of regret and longing.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left. I have not spoken your name in so long, not even to myself, for fear it would bring the pain I have buried deep. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the lighthouse stands alone against the fury of the world, I find I cannot hold back the truth any longer.

I have always been the keeper of this light, the guardian of the shore, the one who watches over the lost and the lonely. But I have never been able to watch over you. I have failed you, Eleanor. I have failed to understand. I have failed to be the father you deserved.

You were so young when you left, so full of fire and fury that I did not know how to temper. You said I was too strict, too distant, too afraid of the dark. But I was not afraid of the dark, my child—I was afraid of what I might find if I let you in. I was afraid of the truth of my own heart, and I chose to shut it away.

Now, as the sea rises and the wind screams

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1897**

**Lighthouse, Northumberland**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit against the stone walls of this old tower. The sea roars with fury, and the sky is a swirling mass of black clouds, heavy with rain and thunder. I have not slept in days, and I fear that tonight may be my last. Yet, even in the face of death, I must write to you—because you are the only thing that has kept me going in these years of silence.

Five long years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of cold stares from the villagers who whisper about you. I have tried to forget, but the memory of your last words—your cold, unyielding gaze—has haunted me like the storm that now rages outside.

I know you think I failed you. That I chose duty over family. But I did not choose to forget you. I chose to protect you. I chose to believe that the world would be better if you were not here. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was wrong to think that distance would heal what was broken between us.

Tonight, I am not sure I will survive. The sea has claimed many a man before me, and I have no illusions about my fate. But if I do not live to see the dawn, I want you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink smudged from the salt air and the tremor of a hand that has long since forgotten its steadiness. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this house, that cold, empty house, and the sea has whispered your name in the dark every night since. I have not spoken to you, not once, and yet I have not forgotten you. You are the ghost that haunts my lamp, the shadow that flickers in the beam when the fog rolls in. You are the reason I keep the light burning, even when the world is silent.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I have seen the sky turn the color of old blood, the way the wind screams like a beast with a broken spine. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have felt the weight of the sea pressing against the walls. I do not know if I will make it through the night, my dear. I have not slept in days, and my bones ache with the burden of this place, this lonely duty, this endless vigil. But I will not let the light go out—not while there is still a chance you might return.

You remember how we used to sit by the fire, you and I, and talk of the stars and the endless horizon? You used to call them the "eyes of the gods," and I would smile and say that the sea was their reflection. You were so

**April 12th, 1889**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea has grown restless, and I fear the storm that gathers on the horizon may be more than nature’s wrath—it may be the end of me.

Five years have passed since you left, and though the days have dragged on like lead, I have never forgotten you. I have kept your name in my heart, and the nights, when the lighthouse burns low and the waves crash against the cliffs, I think of you more than I should. I have tried to be a man of reason, of duty, of discipline—but the sea has always been my only true companion, and it speaks to me in ways that no man, not even a father, can ever truly understand.

You were always too young to understand the weight of the world, and I was too blind to see that you were carrying it on your shoulders. I was a fool to think I could shield you from the storms of life. I was a fool to believe that love could be measured in words and letters, that I could ever make up for the years I lost.

But tonight, as the sky darkens and the wind screams through the tower, I find myself thinking of you again. I have spent my life watching the light, guiding ships through the darkest nights, and I have always believed that the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a banshee, rattling the shutters of this old keep, and the sea roars as if it too seeks to tear down the walls of this crumbling fortress. I have not seen the light of day in days, and the storm is not merely a tempest—it is a reckoning. I fear I may not see dawn.

Five years have passed since you last set foot in this place, and five years have done little to mend the rift between us. I have heard the whispers of your life in London, of your fine gowns and your society soirées, your husband’s name on your lips like a promise. But I do not care for finery, Eliza. I care for the sea, for the light, for the quiet dignity of a life lived in service to something greater than oneself.

You left, as you always have, with your sharp tongue and your haughty eyes, as if I were a man who had failed you in some grand and tragic way. But you do not understand. This life is not for the faint of heart. The sea does not forgive, nor does it forget. It does not bend to the whims of those who think themselves above it.

I have seen the storms that would break lesser men. I have stood alone, night after night, with only the beacon and my

**April 3rd, 1897**  
**Lighthouse, North Sea**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that gathers on the horizon. Five years have passed since you left, and yet I still hear your laughter echoing through these stone halls. The sea has never been kinder to me than it has been in this time of sorrow. It has taken my silence, my sanity, and perhaps, my very soul.

You were always a tempest in your own right, my child. I have spent these years watching the waves crash against the cliffs, wondering if you were ever truly my daughter, or merely a passing shadow of the woman I once dreamed you would become. I have watched the lighthouse burn, and I have watched the light return, and I have watched the world spin on, indifferent to the pain of a father and a son.

Tonight, the sky is black as a widow’s shroud, and the wind howls like a banshee. The sea is rising, and I fear it may take me before the dawn. I have not slept in days, and I do not know if I will live to see the sun rise again. But if I do, I will not be the same man who once stood in the tower, watching over the waves with pride in his heart.

You were my light, Eleanor. The light that once shone in my life, guiding me through

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heavy heart, for the storm that approaches is not merely a tempest of wind and rain, but a storm of sorrow and regret. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and though I have continued my duty as lighthouse keeper, I have felt the weight of your absence more than ever. The light I keep burning is but a pale reflection of the warmth I once shared with you.

Tonight, the sky has darkened with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The sea, once a constant companion, now roars with a voice that seems to echo the pain I carry within. I have been watching the horizon for hours, and I fear that this storm may be the end of all things. I have no illusions—my time is limited, and I would not have you bear the burden of my passing in silence.

I have often thought of you, Eleanor. Of the days we spent together, of the laughter that filled our home, and of the dreams we once shared. You were the light of my life, and I have been a fool to let it fade. I know you do not wish to hear from me, and I do not blame you. But if there is any chance that you may still care, I must speak.

I have no grand words, no elaborate sentiments to offer. Only the truth, as it is. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I have spent the last five years in quiet despair, longing for the day I might once again speak to you, yet the silence between us has grown heavier than the fog that clings to these shores. Tonight, as I stand before the lighthouse, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with fury, I feel the weight of the storm not only in the air but in my very bones. I fear I may not see dawn.

It is said that the sea has no memory, but I have always believed it does—of every storm, every ship lost, every soul who has ever stood where I now stand. Tonight, I feel the storm is not merely a natural phenomenon, but a reckoning. A reminder of the choices I have made, the paths I have walked, and the ones I have forsaken. I have spent my life ensuring the safety of those who sail these waters, yet I have failed to protect the most important relationship of all—the one between a father and his daughter.

I have often wondered if you remember the nights we spent by the fire, the stories I told you of the sea, of mermaids and ghost ships, of brave sailors and their fates. Those were the days when our hearts were still connected, even if our words were not. Now, I fear those memories are fading, like the light from the lighthouse

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. Five years have passed since I last saw you, and though time has moved forward, it feels as if I have been frozen in place, waiting for you to return. I have not spoken to you in this time, not because I do not wish to, but because I feared my words would not reach you, or worse, that they would be received in the wrong way.

You were so young when you left—just a girl, with dreams that stretched beyond the cliffs of this lonely lighthouse. I remember the way you looked at the sea, as if it held secrets only you could understand. I remember the way you called me "father," and how I felt both proud and jealous of the bond you shared with the ocean. It was not that I did not want you to grow, but that I feared what the world would do to you. I feared what I might become.

And now, as I sit here in this lighthouse, the storm is coming. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it too is crying out. I have spent my life watching the waves, learning their language, and ensuring that no ship is lost to the dark. But tonight, I fear that even I may not be enough. The storm is fierce, and I am old. My body is failing me, and I do not know if

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that looms beyond these walls. Five years have passed since you last set foot in this house, and yet it feels like only yesterday that you stormed out, your eyes blazing with anger and your voice ringing through the halls like a curse. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even in my prayers.

The wind howls now, a sound that mirrors the tempest within me. I have always believed that the sea is the most faithful of all things—its tides rising and falling with unerring precision, its waves never ceasing in their eternal dance. But tonight, it seems even the sea has turned against me. The sky is dark as ink, the air thick with the scent of rain and impending ruin. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night since the day I took this post, but I fear the light may not be enough to guide us through what is to come.

You were always so quick to judge, Eleanor. You saw only the loneliness, the solitude, the endless nights of duty that left me with nothing but the cold stone walls and the flickering glow of the lamp. You did not see the quiet moments—when the storm had passed, and the sea was calm, and I would sit by the window, watching the horizon, hoping for a sign that you might one day return. You did not see the way I

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that now churns beyond these stone walls. The sea howls like a beast in pain, and the wind howls louder still, as if the very sky is weeping. I do not know if I shall see another dawn. But if I do, I must speak to you—though I fear my words may be too late.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not dared to write. Not because I do not wish to, but because I fear the words may not be enough. I have spent these years watching the horizon, expecting to see your face in the mist, but I have only seen the sea. And the sea, my dear, is a cruel and unrelenting mistress.

I know you think me a man of stone, unmoved by the world’s sorrows. But I am not. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the words we spoke that last night. You said I had no heart, that I was cold, that I did not love you. I did not answer. I could not. I was too afraid that if I did, I might break you further.

But now, as the storm gathers, I find myself remembering the way you used to sit by the light, watching the waves, as if searching for something in the dark. You were always so curious, always asking questions about the sea, the stars, the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

The wind howls like a banshee tonight, and the sea roars with a fury that even the mightiest of men would fear. I sit here by the light, the great beacon that has guided ships through countless storms, and I feel the weight of the world pressing down on me. It’s not the storm, my love—not the crashing waves or the darkness that swallows the sky. It’s the silence. The silence of your absence, the silence that has grown heavier with each passing year.

I’ve been thinking of you, Eleanor. Not just the girl I once knew, but the woman you’ve become. I’ve not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I ever will again. But tonight, as the storm rages and the lighthouse trembles beneath the fury of the sea, I feel the need to speak. To say the things I’ve kept buried in my chest for too long.

You were my light once, Eleanor. Not the lighthouse, but you. You were the calm in the storm, the steady hand that held me together when the world felt too cruel. I remember the nights you used to come to visit, even though you never said why. You’d sit by the light with me, and we’d watch the waves crash against the rocks, and for a moment, the world would still. I used to think that was love—those quiet moments, the way your eyes reflected

My dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart that aches with more than mere sorrow. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars as if it too is weeping. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of silence more than ever.

You do not know how often I have sat by the light, watching the waves crash against the cliffs, wondering if I have been a fool to let you go. I have not written to you, not once. Not since the day you left, with your mother’s tears and your own hardened gaze. I have tried to forget, to bury the pain, but it lingers like a ghost in the shadows of this lighthouse. And now, as the storm approaches, I fear it may be too late to reach you.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child who believed she knew what was right, who believed she could leave without a trace. But I have watched you grow from that girl into a woman who no longer speaks to me. I have seen the way you look at me now, not with love, but with something colder—something I cannot name.

I have always been the keeper of this light, the one who watches over the sea, the one who guides ships safely home. But I have never been able to guide you back. I have tried to be the father

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea roars and the sky is a bruised shade of black, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I have not seen you in five years, and in that time, the world has turned, and I have remained here, alone, a sentinel of the waves, watching over the lives of others while my own has crumbled in silence.

I have tried to reach you, Eleanor. I have sent letters that were returned, marked *no address*, or *unknown*. I have waited for a reply, for a word, for even a sign that you still remember me. But you have not answered. And I have not dared to write again, for fear that my words might be too much, or that you might see them and turn away once more.

Tonight, the storm is coming. It is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The waves are rising, the wind is screaming, and I know that I may not make it through the night. If I do not, I would have one last thing to say to you—before the sea takes me.

I have always believed that the light of the lighthouse is a beacon not just for ships, but for the lost souls who wander the world, searching for something to believe in. I have always believed that you, too, were

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the ink smudged from the dampness of the sea air that slithers through the cracks in the lighthouse walls. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves crash against the rocks below with a fury that makes my bones ache. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. Five long, heart-wrenching years. I have not spoken to you, nor have I seen your face, nor heard your voice. But tonight, as the storm rages, I feel the weight of that silence pressing down on me like the very sea that surrounds this lonely tower.

I suppose you think me a monster, a man who abandoned you. Perhaps you do. And I would not blame you. I was a fool, Eliza. A fool who let pride and shame blind me to the truth of my own heart. I was too proud to beg for your forgiveness. Too proud to ask for your return. But tonight, as I sit here in the dim light of the lantern, I wonder if I would have been willing to do so if I had known the storm would take me before I could.

You were only ten when you left. I remember the day like it was yesterday—your small hand clutching the doorframe as you stood on the threshold, eyes wide with fear and sorrow. You looked at me then, and I saw the same look in your eyes

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters of this old lighthouse, and I fear the night may claim me before I can finish these words. But if the sea takes me, I wish to leave you something—something more than the hollow echoes of my absence.

Five years have passed since you left, and five years of nights like this, where the waves roar like the voices of the dead. I’ve kept the light burning, as I always have, for those who sail the dark waters. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I wonder if it is the sea that seeks me—or the past.

You were once my light, Eleanor. I remember the way you used to run up the spiral stairs, eyes wide with wonder at the beacon’s glow. You would ask me, "Why do we need the light, Papa?" and I would tell you, "Because the sea is cruel, and the night is blind. We must guide those who are lost."

But you were not lost, my child. You were found by other hands, and I was left behind. I do not know what you saw in the world beyond this shore, nor do I care to know. You chose your path, and I chose mine. We both made our choices, and the sea has taken its toll on both our hearts.

I have not spoken

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in the best of health, though I daresay it will not find you in the best of spirits. I write to you now with the weight of the world upon my shoulders, and the wind howling like a banshee in the night. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just my lamp and my sanity.

It has been five years since we last spoke, and I have not heard a word from you since you left this house, that once proud and loving home, now reduced to a hollow shell. I have tried, Eleanor, I have truly tried, to reach you. I wrote, I called, I even sent a letter to your mother, begging her to intervene. But you have turned your back on us, as though we were strangers or worse—though I do not know what could have driven you to such a cruel fate.

I have spent these years alone, with only the sea and the ever-watchful light to keep me company. The lamp burns bright, as it always has, though I fear it may not be enough to ward off the darkness that now threatens to consume me. Each night, I sit by the window, staring into the abyss, wondering if you ever look up at the lighthouse and see the light that once guided your way home.

The storm is worse than any I have seen in my years here. The waves crash against the cliffs like the fury

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, for the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars as if it would devour the very stones of this cliff. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not live to see the dawn. But if I do, I must speak to you—though I know you may not wish to hear from me.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. I suppose I should not be surprised. You were always the spirited one, the one who longed for more than the quiet life of this lighthouse. You were not content with the sea, the waves, or the stars. You wanted *adventure*. I tried to dissuade you, as any good father would, but you were too young, too headstrong, too... *Eleanor*.

You were my only child, and I loved you more than life itself. But I see now that I failed you. I failed to understand you, to see the fire in your heart, the hunger for the world beyond these rocks. You left, as I feared you would, and I did not stop you. I should have. I should have taken you by the shoulders and said, "Eleanor, this is not the life for you. Stay here with me. We can be a family."

But you were gone before I could say it.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I fear the storm that now roars upon the cliffs will not be the last I face. I have been the keeper of this lighthouse for over thirty years, and though I have weathered many tempests, none have ever felt as dire as the one that now approaches.

I have not seen you in ages, and I do not know if you will ever forgive me for the silence that has stretched between us. I have been a man of duty, of routine, and of duty above all. The sea has always been my companion, and the light has always been my purpose. But I have come to see that I have neglected the one thing that truly matters: my family.

You were but a child when I last held you in my arms, and I have since lost sight of the girl you once were. I have heard from the village that you have taken up with the wrong sort of people, and that you have left the town where I have lived all my life. I do not know if it is true, but I fear it may be. I have no wish to be the cause of your ruin, yet I have no voice to call you back.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write these words with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I know that the storm that now rages against our shores may claim me before I can reach you. The winds howl like the wails of the damned, and the sea, in its fury, seems to rise as if to swallow the very land we call home. I have spent a lifetime standing upon this cliff, guiding ships through the darkest nights, but never have I felt so lost as I do now, knowing that my time may be at an end.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, I have come to believe that silence has become our only companion. I have tried, dear Eleanor, to reach out, to mend the rift that has grown between us, but the distance—both physical and emotional—has proven too great. I have sent letters, each one filled with the hopes of a father longing for the love of his child, yet they have gone unanswered. I wonder if you have forgotten me, or if the world has buried your memory beneath the weight of its own burdens.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper’s duty is to light the way for others, to be a beacon of hope in the darkest of times. But tonight, I find myself questioning whether my light has ever truly reached you. I have stood here for so many years, watching the waves, waiting for a sign that you might one day return

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of being heard, but with the desperation of a man who has spent his life standing alone on this rock, watching the sea, and who now finds himself staring into the abyss of his own heart.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and I have not written to you in that time. I have not spoken to you. And yet, I have thought of you every single day. I have thought of you in the quiet hours of the night, when the wind howls like a banshee and the waves crash against the cliffs like the memories I cannot let go of. I have thought of you as I stood here, in the cold, with the light of the lighthouse burning bright, and I have wondered if you would ever come back to see it. If you would ever come back to see me.

You left, Eleanor, and you left without a word. I do not know if it was because you were angry, or because you were hurt, or because you simply did not know how to stay. I do not know if you ever meant to return, or if you ever thought of me at all. But I have stayed. I have stayed because I have a duty, a responsibility, and a promise to those who depend on me. **My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink of desperation, my hands trembling like the sea outside. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves claw at the cliffs with a fury that makes me think the world itself is ending. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet here I am, with the storm upon us, and my heart feels as though it might shatter before the dawn.

You were ten when you left. I remember the day as if it were yesterday—the sky was gray, the air heavy with the scent of impending rain, and you stood at the door, your small frame wrapped in a shawl too large for you, your eyes glistening with unshed tears. You looked at me then, and I saw in your face the same sorrow I had carried since the day your mother passed. You said nothing, only turned and walked away, and I did not chase you. I did not know how to reach you, and I feared that if I did, I would lose you forever.

Five years have passed, and I have tried to forget. I have tried to fill the silence with the sound of the sea, the work of the light, the solitude of the tower. But the silence is louder than the waves, and the memories are sharper than the salt in the air. I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I saw you, and though I have not spoken, I have not forgotten. You are the only thing that has kept me from losing myself in the endless nights, the only light that has ever guided me through the dark.

Tonight, a storm is coming—terrible, as if the sea itself is weeping. I have spent my life watching the horizon, but never have I seen such fury in the sky. The waves are rising, the winds are howling, and I fear that I may not see another dawn. But if I do not, I want you to know that I have not forgotten you. I have not stopped thinking of you, even when the world felt too vast and too lonely.

I have often wondered what you are doing now. I imagine you are growing, changing, becoming someone else. I have heard of the city, of the great streets and the bustling life. I have read of the world beyond this rock and the sea, and I have longed to see it. But I have remained here, bound to this lighthouse, because I believe that some things are worth staying for. Some things are worth holding on to, even when the world moves on.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. Time has worn away the edges of our words, the silence between us heavier than any storm. I thought I could outrun it, but the sea never lets you go, and neither does the weight of what we left unsaid.

You were always the light in my life, my little star, and I have been a fool to let it fade. I thought I could protect you, shelter you from the cruel winds of this world, but I was wrong. I have been blind, deaf to the pain you carried, and I have let my pride keep me from reaching out. I should have been there when you needed me most. I should have held you when the world felt too heavy.

Five years. Five years since you last called me *father*. Five years since you left this house, this life, and this man behind you. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even in my dreams, for fear that if I did, the silence would shatter.

**Letter from a Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*October 12th, 1897*  
*The cliffs of Skerry Point, Isle of Wight*

It carries every whisper, every echo of what was, and tonight, as the storm gathers, I feel it more than ever.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the sea howling at my window, the wind tearing at the shutters like a vengeful spirit. The sky is a bruised shade of black, and the waves have grown so fierce they seem to rise up in defiance of the very stars. I do not know if I shall see morning. But I must write, for there is something I have not said in five years, and I fear I may never get the chance.

You were but a girl when you left, with a suitcase and a heart full of dreams. I watched you walk through the door, your steps light, your eyes bright with the fire of youth. I told you I loved you, and you said you did too. But the words felt like stones in my chest. You did not stay, and I did not call you back. I did not call you back because I thought I was protecting you from the truth.

I have always been a man of duty. The lighthouse, the sea, the light—it is all I have ever known. You were my light, Eleanor. I tried to be a father to you, but I failed. I did not understand the world you were growing into, nor the heart you were building. I was too busy watching the horizon, too consumed by the rhythm of the waves. I did not see the girl I loved slipping away, and when I finally did, it was too late.
